title: Add elp-profile wrapper for profiling ogent functions
description:
Create a profiling utility that wraps Emacs's built-in ELP profiler.

**Why**: From elisp-handbook.org - ELP profiling shows exactly which functions are slow and how many times they're called.

**Implementation**:

```elisp
;;; ogent-profile.el --- Profiling utilities -*- lexical-binding: t; -*-

(require 'elp)

(defvar ogent-profile-functions
  '(ogent-context-build
    ogent-context--node-from-element
    ogent-resolve-handle
    ogent-edit-parse-response
    ogent-companion-get-or-create)
  "Functions to instrument for profiling.")

(defun ogent-profile-start ()
  "Start profiling ogent functions."
  (interactive)
  (elp-instrument-list ogent-profile-functions)
  (message "Profiling %d ogent functions" (length ogent-profile-functions)))

(defun ogent-profile-stop ()
  "Stop profiling and show results."
  (interactive)
  (elp-results)
  (elp-restore-all))

(defmacro ogent-with-profile (&rest body)
  "Execute BODY with profiling enabled, then show results."
  `(progn
     (ogent-profile-start)
     (unwind-protect
         (progn ,@body)
       (ogent-profile-stop))))
```

**Example output**:
```
| Function                      | Times called | Total time   | Average time |
| ogent-context-build           |           30 | 0.0036004130 | 0.0001200137 |
| ogent-resolve-handle          |           10 | 2.089...e-05 | 2.089...e-06 |
```

**Reference**: elisp-handbook.org lines 3240-3280

title: Add GitHub Actions CI workflow
description:
Set up continuous integration testing with GitHub Actions.

**Workflow Features**:
1. Test on multiple Emacs versions (27.2, 28.2, 29.1, snapshot)
2. Run on push to master and pull requests
3. Separate lint and test steps
4. Use makem.sh with sandbox mode
5. Install dependencies automatically

**Implementation** (`.github/workflows/test.yml`):
```yaml
name: CI
on:
  pull_request:
  push:
    branches: [master]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        emacs_version:
          - 27.2
          - 28.2
          - 29.1
          - snapshot
    steps:
      - uses: purcell/setup-emacs@master
        with:
          version: ${{ matrix.emacs_version }}
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Initialize sandbox
        run: |
          SANDBOX_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
          echo "SANDBOX_DIR=$SANDBOX_DIR" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          ./makem.sh -vv --sandbox=$SANDBOX_DIR --install-deps --install-linters
      - name: Lint
        run: ./makem.sh -vv --sandbox=$SANDBOX_DIR lint
      - name: Test
        if: always()
        run: ./makem.sh -vv --sandbox=$SANDBOX_DIR test
```

**Depends on**: ogent-ole (makem.sh adoption)

**Reference**: https://github.com/alphapapa/ement.el/blob/master/.github/workflows/test.yml

title: Use cl-flet/cl-letf for mocking in tests
description:
Use `cl-letf` to temporarily override functions during testing.

**Why**: From elisp-handbook.org - `cl-letf` allows temporarily rebinding function definitions, which is essential for unit testing functions that depend on external services.

**Pattern**:
```elisp
;; Mock gptel-request to avoid actual API calls
(ert-deftest ogent-request-sends-correct-payload ()
  "Test that ogent-request formats the request correctly."
  (let ((captured-args nil))
    (cl-letf (((symbol-function 'gptel-request)
               (lambda (&rest args)
                 (setq captured-args args)
                 'mock-response)))
      (ogent-request "test prompt")
      (should captured-args)
      (should (string= (car captured-args) "test prompt")))))

;; Mock network functions
(ert-deftest ogent-onboard-verify-handles-errors ()
  "Test verification handles API errors gracefully."
  (cl-letf (((symbol-function 'gptel-make-anthropic)
             (lambda (&rest _)
               (error "Connection refused"))))
    (should-not (ogent-onboard--verify-connection provider key model))))
```

**Current tests that could benefit**:
- `ogent-core-tests.el` - testing request flow without network
- `ogent-ui-tests.el` - testing UI without gptel backend
- Streaming simulation tests (ogent-2e7)

**Note**: For Buttercup users, this is built-in as `spy-on`.

**Reference**: elisp-handbook.org lines 186-192

```bash
./makem.sh --sandbox=/tmp/ogent-sandbox --install-deps

# Run tests in sandbox

# Interactive sandbox session
```

```makefile
# Run interactive Emacs with ogent in sandbox

```

**Reference**: https://github.com/alphapapa/makem.sh#sandbox

title: Create CONTRIBUTING.md with development workflow
description:
Document how to set up a development environment and contribute to ogent.

**Sections to include**:

## Getting Started
- Clone repository
- Install dependencies (gptel, transient, org)
- Run `make test` to verify setup
- Run `make lint` to check code style

## Development Workflow
- Use `make sandbox` to test in isolated Emacs
- Use `make batch` to run Emacs with project loaded
- Run tests frequently: `make test`

## Testing
- Test files go in `test/` as `*-tests.el`
- Use ERT for unit tests
- Run single test: `make test-ert TEST=ogent-edit-parse-single-block`
- Add tests for all new functionality

## Linting
- `make lint` runs all linters
- `make lint-checkdoc` for documentation
- `make lint-compile` for byte-compilation warnings
- `make lint-package` for MELPA compliance

## Interactive Debugging with Edebug
```elisp
;; Instrument a function for debugging
C-u C-M-x   ; on defun to instrument
;; In Edebug:
SPC         ; step forward
n           ; next expression
c           ; continue to end
i           ; step into function
e           ; eval expression
q           ; quit
```

## Code Style
- Use lexical-binding in all files
- Prefix all public symbols with `ogent-`
- Private symbols use `ogent--` (double dash)
- Follow Emacs Lisp conventions

## Pull Request Process
1. Fork and create feature branch
2. Add tests for new functionality
3. Run `make all` (lint + test)
4. Submit PR with clear description

**API**:
```elisp
(ogent-edit-parse-response RESPONSE-TEXT SOURCE-BUFFER)

  file buffer
  start-line end-line

```

**Edge cases**:

title: API integration for tool_use protocol
description:
Extend the API client to support Anthropic's tool_use format:
- Send tool definitions with requests
- Parse tool_use content blocks from responses
- Handle tool_result submission back to API
- Support multi-turn tool conversations (assistant→tool_use→user(tool_result)→assistant)
design:
**Leverage gptel's FSM:**

gptel already handles tool_use protocol via its FSM (WAIT→TYPE→TOOL→DONE states). ogent currently bypasses this with a simple callback.

**Integration approach:**
1. Study `gptel-send--handlers` FSM implementation
2. Hook into TOOL state transitions instead of reimplementing
3. When FSM enters TOOL state, call ogent's org-mode rendering
4. Use gptel's `gptel--handle-tool-use` for execution
5. Feed tool results back through gptel's protocol

**Key functions to integrate:**
- `gptel--update-tool-call` - displays pending tool calls
- `gptel--handle-tool-use` - executes confirmed tools  
- `gptel--update-tool-ask` - prompts for acceptance

This is adaptation work, not new protocol implementation.

title: gptel integration analysis (research complete)
description:
**Research findings captured - reference for implementation**

## Key Discovery
gptel already has comprehensive tool call support that ogent can leverage:
- `gptel-make-tool` for tool definitions
- FSM with TOOL state handling
- `#+begin_tool` org block format (ogent already uses this)
- `ogent-ui--insert-tool-block` exists but isn't wired to FSM

## What to Reuse
- `gptel-request` - HTTP/streaming (already using)
- `gptel-make-preset` with `:tools` field
- `gptel--handle-tool-use` for execution
- `gptel--display-tool-calls/results` patterns
- FSM state machine structure

## What to Build
- Wire gptel FSM TOOL state → ogent org rendering
- Add `:tools` to `ogent-model-registry`
- Org-centric confirmation UX
- Tool execution context with buffer access

## Relevant Code Locations
- `ogent-ui--insert-tool-block` (line 825) - exists, needs FSM wiring
- `ogent-ui--insert-reasoning-block` (line 841) - exists, needs streaming
- `ogent-ui--make-callback` (line 643) - current simple callback to extend

```elisp
(defcustom ogent-edit-display-method 'smerge
  "Method for displaying proposed edits.
Options: 'smerge (built-in), 'inline-diff (requires package)."
```

title: Adopt makem.sh for build automation
description:
Replace custom lint.el with makem.sh for standardized build, lint, and test automation.

**Why makem.sh**:
- Zero-config: works without initialization for most packages
- Sandbox mode: isolated testing with `--sandbox` flag
- Multi-Emacs support: test against different versions
- CI-ready: includes GitHub Actions workflow template
- Comprehensive: lint, compile, test in one tool
- Proven: used by ement.el, org-ql, and many alphapapa packages

**Implementation**:
1. Download makem.sh to project root
2. Update Makefile to delegate to makem.sh (like ement.el pattern)
3. Keep lint.el for backwards compatibility initially
4. Add `--install-deps` and `--install-linters` support

**Makefile targets after adoption**:
```makefile
# All targets delegate to makem.sh
%:
    @./makem.sh $(VERBOSE) $(SANDBOX) $(@)

# Common shortcuts
lint test compile all: ; # implicitly handled by %
```

**Available makem.sh rules**:
- `all` - Run all lints and tests
- `compile` - Byte-compile source files
- `lint` - Run all linters
- `lint-checkdoc` - Check documentation conventions
- `lint-compile` - Byte-compile with warnings as errors
- `lint-package` - Run package-lint
- `lint-regexps` - Run relint for regex issues
- `test` - Run all tests (ERT and Buttercup)
- `batch` / `interactive` - Run Emacs with project loaded

**Reference**: https://github.com/alphapapa/makem.sh

title: Consider Buttercup for BDD-style tests
description:
Evaluate migrating from ERT to Buttercup for more expressive tests.

**Why consider Buttercup**:
- BDD-style: `describe` / `it` structure is more readable
- Better organization with nested `describe` blocks
- Built-in spy/mock functionality
- Setup/teardown with `before-each` / `after-each`
- Pending tests with `xit` / `xdescribe`
- Works with makem.sh (`test-buttercup` rule)

**Example comparison**:

ERT (current):
```elisp
(ert-deftest ogent-edit-parse-single-block ()
  "Parse a single SEARCH/REPLACE block."
  (let* ((response "<<<<<<< SEARCH\nold\n=======\nnew\n>>>>>>> REPLACE")
         (edits (ogent-edit-parse-response response buf)))
    (should (= (length edits) 1))
    (should (string= (ogent-edit-old-text (car edits)) "old"))))
```

Buttercup:
```elisp
(describe "ogent-edit-parse-response"
  (describe "with single SEARCH/REPLACE block"
    (it "extracts one edit"
      (let ((edits (ogent-edit-parse-response response buf)))
        (expect (length edits) :to-equal 1)))
    
    (it "captures the old text"
      (let ((edits (ogent-edit-parse-response response buf)))
        (expect (ogent-edit-old-text (car edits)) :to-equal "old")))))
```

**Decision factors**:
- Migration cost vs benefit
- Team familiarity
- Integration complexity
- CI compatibility (already supported by makem.sh)

**Recommendation**: Keep ERT for now, but structure tests to be migration-friendly. Consider Buttercup for new complex test suites where mocking is needed.

**Reference**: https://github.com/jorgenschaefer/emacs-buttercup/

title: Optimize string insertion using concat+insert pattern
description:
Use `(insert (apply #'concat strings))` instead of multiple insert calls.

**Why**: From elisp-handbook.org benchmarks, calling insert once with concatenated strings is **6x faster** than calling insert multiple times.

**Benchmark from handbook**:
```
| (insert (apply #'concat ... | 100 | 0.000142085 |
| (apply #'insert ...         | 100 | 0.000678471 |
| (loop do (insert ...        | 100 | 0.000869779 |
```

**Locations to optimize**:

1. `ogent-codemap.el` - `ogent-codemap--insert-file`:
```elisp
;; Before
(insert (format "** [[file:%s][%s]]\n" relative relative))
(dolist (def defs)
  (insert (format "*** [[file:%s::%s][%s]]\n" relative def def)))

;; After
(insert 
 (apply #'concat
  (format "** [[file:%s][%s]]\n" relative relative)
  (mapcar (lambda (def)
            (format "*** [[file:%s::%s][%s]]\n" relative def def))
          defs)))
```

2. `ogent-context.el` - formatting functions that build strings
3. `ogent-edit-log.el` - logging output that writes to buffers

**Trade-off**: Code readability vs performance. Only apply where inserting many strings in a loop.

**Reference**: elisp-handbook.org lines 288-330

description: Preserve manual annotations when regenerating codemap. Track file modification times to only rescan changed files. Use markers or IDs to identify existing entries.

title: Consider plz.el for HTTP requests instead of url.el
description:
Evaluate using plz.el for any direct HTTP needs (API health checks, etc.).

**Why**: From elisp-handbook.org:
- `plz` is more reliable than `url.el` (which has obscure bugs)
- Simple, natural API
- Available on GNU ELPA (default installable)
- Supports both sync and async requests
- Better error handling

**Current situation**: ogent relies on gptel for API communication, so this may not be directly needed. However, useful for:
- API health checks in onboarding
- Token counting API calls
- Future direct API features

**Comparison**:
```elisp
;; url.el (verbose, callback-based)
(url-retrieve "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/health"
              (lambda (status)
                (when-let ((err (plist-get status :error)))
                  (error "Request failed: %s" err))
                ...))

;; plz (cleaner, sync or async)
(plz 'get "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/health"
  :as 'json
  :then (lambda (response) ...))

;; Or sync
(plz-get-sync "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/health" :as 'json)
```

**Decision**: Research task - evaluate if ogent has HTTP needs beyond gptel.

**Package**: https://github.com/alphapapa/plz.el

**Reference**: elisp-handbook.org lines 1768-1805

title: Implement smerge-based edit display
description:
Display proposed edits in source buffer using smerge conflict markers.

**Requirements**:
1. Insert conflict markers around proposed changes
2. Enable smerge-mode in affected buffers
3. Position cursor at first change
4. Highlight the modified regions

**Implementation**:
```elisp
(defun ogent-edit-apply-as-smerge (edit)
  "Apply EDIT to source buffer as smerge conflict."
  ;; Find OLD text location
  ;; Replace with conflict markers:
  ;; <<<<<<< original
  ;; old-text
  ;; =======
  ;; new-text
  ;; >>>>>>> ogent
  ;; Enable smerge-mode
  )
```

**User workflow**:
- `smerge-keep-other` (C-c ^ o) to accept AI changes
- `smerge-keep-mine` (C-c ^ m) to reject
- `smerge-next` / `smerge-prev` to navigate

**Inspiration**: Elysium package uses this approach
